The ACTG 320 dataset is derived from a randomized, double-blind clinical trial evaluating the efficacy of combination antiretroviral therapy in HIV-infected patients with advanced disease (CD4 cell counts ≤ 200 cells/mm³). Participants were randomly assigned to receive either a two-drug nucleoside regimen or a three-drug regimen that included the protease inhibitor indinavir. The dataset contains individual-level observations, including treatment assignment, baseline clinical characteristics (e.g., CD4 count, prior antiretroviral therapy), demographic variables, and follow-up information on clinical outcomes. A total of 1,156 patients participated in the trial but only 96 experienced progression to AIDS or death during follow-up. Subgroups with smaller sample sizes would therefore contribute disproportionately fewer events, limiting the ability to detect differences in treatment effects across demographic groups.

The primary outcome is a time-to-event variable defined as progression to AIDS or death, along with a censoring indicator reflecting whether the event was observed during the study period. The dataset also includes subgroup-defining variables (e.g., sex and race/ethnicity), which allow for investigation of treatment effect heterogeneity and, importantly for this study, assessment of how reduced subgroup sample sizes and event counts impact statistical power.

A formal prospective power analysis for subgroup effects was not reported in the original ACTG 320 study. This is consistent with standard clinical trial design, where sample size calculations are typically based on detecting treatment effects in the overall study population rather than within demographic subgroups. In survival analysis, power is driven by the number of observed events, which may differ substantially from initial assumptions and can vary across subgroups.
